data.description: The FIX (Financial Information Exchange) Protocol is an industry standard used by financial institutions to… guess what… exchange financial information. A FIX message is simply a sequence of key-value pairs, each followed by a SOH (ASCII value 1) character. Since SOH is a non-printable character, FIX messages are often illustrated using another character, such as a … Continue reading Calculating the Checksum of a FIX Message →

<article id="post-838" class="post-838 post type-post status-publish format-standard hentry category-software-development tag-fix-protocol">
	
	<header class="entry-header">
				<div class="entry-meta">
			<span class="cat-links"><a href="https://gigi.nullneuron.net/gigilabs/category/software-development/" rel="category tag">Software development</a></span>
		</div>
		<h1 class="entry-title">Calculating the Checksum of a FIX Message</h1>
		<div class="entry-meta">
			<span class="entry-date"><a href="https://gigi.nullneuron.net/gigilabs/calculating-the-checksum-of-a-fix-message/" rel="bookmark"><time class="entry-date" datetime="2015-10-17T12:43:58+02:00">October 17, 2015</time></a></span> <span class="byline"><span class="author vcard"><a class="url fn n" href="https://gigi.nullneuron.net/gigilabs/author/gigi/" rel="author">Gigi</a></span></span>			<span class="comments-link"><a href="https://gigi.nullneuron.net/gigilabs/calculating-the-checksum-of-a-fix-message/#comments">2 Comments</a></span>
					</div><!-- .entry-meta -->
	</header><!-- .entry-header -->

		<div class="entry-content">
		<p>The FIX (Financial Information Exchange) Protocol is an industry standard used by financial institutions to… guess what… exchange financial information.</p>
<p>A FIX message is simply a sequence of key-value pairs, each followed by a SOH (ASCII value 1) character. Since SOH is a non-printable character, FIX messages are often illustrated using another character, such as a pipe. The following is an example of a FIX message, taken from <a href="http://fixparser.targetcompid.com/">FIX Parser</a>:</p>
<p>8=FIX.4.1|9=61|35=A|34=1|49=EXEC|52=20121105-23:24:06|56=BANZAI|98=0|108=30|10=003|</p>
<p>As you can see, key-value pairs are delimited by the pipe character (which would be a SOH character in a real FIX message). Note how there is also a pipe character at the very end. Keys are separated from values with an equals sign (=). The keys themselves are numbers; each number represents a particular field in the FIX protocol. For example, <a href="http://www.onixs.biz/fix-dictionary/4.2/tagNum_8.html">FIX Field 8</a> represents the FIX protocol as well as the beginning of a FIX message. The use of numbers instead of field names makes messages less readable, but keeps them small.</p>
<p>The last field in a FIX message is the checksum (<a href="http://www.onixs.biz/fix-dictionary/4.2/tagNum_10.html">FIX Field 10</a>). The algorithm used to calculate the checksum is described <a href="http://www.onixs.biz/fix-dictionary/4.2/app_b.html">here</a>:</p>
<blockquote><p>“The checksum of a FIX message is calculated by summing every byte of the message up to but not including the checksum field itself. This checksum is then transformed into a modulo 256 number for transmission and comparison. The checksum is calculated after all encryption is completed, i.e. the message as transmitted between parties is processed.</p>
<p>“For transmission, the checksum must be sent as printable characters, so the checksum is transformed into three ASCII digits.</p>
<p>“For example, if the checksum has been calculated to be 274 then the modulo 256 value is 18 (256 + 18 = 274). This value would be transmitted a |10=018| where “10=”is the tag for the checksum field.”</p></blockquote>
<p>In C#, we can implement this as follows.</p>
<p>First, let’s create our test message. We start with a pipe-delimited representation (just because it’s more readable that way), without the checksum field, and then we swap out the pipes for SOH characters (\u0001). Since <a href="http://www.fixtradingcommunity.org/pg/discussions/topicpost/167593/character-encoding-type">FIX uses an ASCII representation</a>, we can easily get a byte representation of the message:</p>
<div><div id="highlighter_878550" class="syntaxhighlighter  csharp"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="gutter"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2">1</div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1">2</div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2">3</div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1">4</div></td><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="csharp keyword">string</code> <code class="csharp plain">message = </code><code class="csharp string">"8=FIX.4.1|9=61|35=A|34=1|49=EXEC|52=20121105-23:24:06|"</code></div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1"><code class="csharp spaces">    </code><code class="csharp plain">+ </code><code class="csharp string">"56=BANZAI|98=0|108=30|"</code><code class="csharp plain">;</code></div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2"><code class="csharp plain">message = message.Replace(</code><code class="csharp string">'|'</code><code class="csharp plain">, </code><code class="csharp string">'\u0001'</code><code class="csharp plain">);</code></div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1"><code class="csharp keyword">byte</code><code class="csharp plain">[] messageBytes = Encoding.ASCII.GetBytes(message);</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div>
<p>The algorithm itself is then easy to implement. We simply iterate over each byte, add its value to a running total, divide it by 256, and take the remainder:</p>
<div><div id="highlighter_78987" class="syntaxhighlighter  csharp"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="gutter"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2">1</div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1">2</div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2">3</div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1">4</div></td><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="csharp keyword">for</code> <code class="csharp plain">(</code><code class="csharp keyword">int</code> <code class="csharp plain">i = 0; i < message.Length; i++)</code></div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1"><code class="csharp spaces">    </code><code class="csharp plain">total += messageBytes[i];</code></div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2"> </div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1"><code class="csharp keyword">int</code> <code class="csharp plain">checksum = total % 256;</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div>
<p>There’s nothing more to add – that’s the checksum. If you’re sending a FIX message, you’ll convert the value to a string and pad it with zeroes if necessary to make sure it’s exactly 3 characters:</p>
<div><div id="highlighter_556539" class="syntaxhighlighter  csharp"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="gutter"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2">1</div></td><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="csharp keyword">string</code> <code class="csharp plain">checksumStr = checksum.ToString().PadLeft(3, </code><code class="csharp string">'0'</code><code class="csharp plain">);</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div>
<p>If you’re parsing a received message, you’ll want to compare the checksum you calculated to the one provided with the message, to make sure the message isn’t corrupt.</p>
	</div><!-- .entry-content -->

metadata.og:description: The FIX (Financial Information Exchange) Protocol is an industry standard used by financial institutions to… guess what… exchange financial information. A FIX message is simply a sequence of key-value pairs, each followed by a SOH (ASCII value 1) character. Since SOH is a non-printable character, FIX messages are often illustrated using another character, such as a … Continue reading Calculating the Checksum of a FIX Message →
